subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 03, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Nasal Continuous Positive Airway Pressure vs Nasal Intermitten
sentences: Story 1 is about Nasal Continuous Positive Airway Pressure vs Nasal Intermittent Positive Pressure Ventilation in Preterm Infants With Respiratory Distress Syndrome: A Randomized Clinical Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in intensive care units in China from December 2021 to October 2024, how should we interpret Nasal Continuous Positive Airway Pressure compared with Nasal Intermittent Positive Pressure Ventilation in Preterm Infants With Respiratory Distress Syndrome: A Randomized Clinical Trial?
A total of 312 preterm infants (median [IQR] gestational age, 28.0 [28.6-29.4] weeks; 174 boys [55.8%]) were randomized to the NCPAP group (153 infants) or the NIPPV group (159 infants).

NIV failure within 72 hours occurred in 40 infants (26.1%) in the NCPAP group vs 21 infants (13.2%) in the NIPPV group (adjusted risk difference, 12.8%; 95% CI, 4.2%-21.6%; P = .004; O'Brien-Fleming adjusted α = .005), exceeding the noninferiority margin and conclusively demonstrating inferiority of NCPAP.
NIV failure within 7 days was also higher in the NCPAP group (42 infants [27.5%] vs 24 infants [15.1%]; risk difference, 12.4%; 95% CI, 3.4%-21.4%; P = .008).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Integrated Telehealth Rehabilitation and Quality of Life in Me
sentences: Story 2 is about Integrated Telehealth Rehabilitation and Quality of Life in Mechanically Ventilated Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from JAMA.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in ICUs of 20 public hospitals in Brazil enrolled adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring invasive mechanical ventilation between June 2024 and May 2025, with follow-up, how should we interpret not differ among survivors (0.60 [0.32] vs 0.59 [0.32]; adjusted difference, -0.045; 95% CI, -0.138 to 0.045; P = .34) compared with usual care, the intervention resulted in lower 90-day all-cause mortality (71.8% [676 of 941] vs 78.3% [584 of 746]; adj?
Among 1916 enrolled patients (mean [SD] age, 60.6 [17.3] years; 43.6% female), 1063 were assigned to the intervention and 853 to usual care per local protocols.

At 90 days after hospital discharge, mean (SD) EQ-5D-3L utility scores were higher in the intervention group than in the usual care group (0.16 [0.31] vs 0.12 [0.28]; adjusted difference, 0.049; 95% CI, 0.0002 to 0.098; P = .04) but did not differ among survivors (0.60 [0.32] vs 0.59 [0.32]; adjusted difference, -0.045; 95% CI, -0.138 to 0.045; P = .34).

Compared with usual care, the intervention resulted in lower 90-day all-cause mortality (71.8% [676 of 941] vs 78.3% [584 of 746]; adjusted difference, -7.6%; 95% CI, -14.7% to -0.6%; P = .03) and shorter mean (SD) mechanical ventilation duration (9.9 [10.3] vs 15.5 [15.9] days; adjusted difference, -6.2 days; 95% CI, -8.5 to -3.9; P < .001).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving st
sentences: Story 3 is about Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards..
This is a recent research article from Intensive care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in critically ill patients, yet the optimal delivery of macronutrient across phases of critical illness remains controversial, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Increasing protein delivery has not consistently improved survival or functional recovery and may even be harmful, especially in patients with acute kidney injury.
Nutritional support is a cornerstone of care in critically ill patients, yet the optimal delivery of macronutrient across phases of critical illness remains controversial.
During the acute phase, profound metabolic alterations including anabolic resistance, enhanced proteolysis and endogenous energy production contribute to rapid muscle wasting.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Nasal Continuous Positive Airway Pressure vs Nasal Intermittent Positive Pressure Ventilation in Preterm Infants With Respiratory Distress Syndrome: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
articles.summary: To determine whether nasal continuous positive airway pressure (NCPAP) is noninferior to nasal intermittent positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) as primary respiratory support before minimally invasive surfactant administration (MISA) for reducing intubation within 72 hours in preterm infants with RDS. The trial was designed to enroll 960 infants but was stopped early after enrolling 312 (32.5% of the target) based on prespecified stopping criteria. Infants were randomized 1:1 to receive NCPAP or NIPPV as initial respiratory support. The primary outcome was NIV failure, defined as requiring intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation within 72 hours after birth. The noninferiority margin was set at a 10% risk difference.
articles.analysis: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

pico.population: intensive care units in China from December 2021 to October 2024
pico.comparator: Nasal Intermittent Positive Pressure Ventilation in Preterm Infants With Respiratory Distress Syndrome: A Randomized Clinical Trial
pico.outcomes: The primary outcome was NIV failure, defined as requiring intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation within 72 hours after birth. The noninferiority margin was set at a 10% risk difference. Secondary outcomes included NIV failure within 7 days, surfactant redosing, and major complications (eg, pneumothorax, bronchopulmonary dysplasia).

keyResults: A total of 312 preterm infants (median [IQR] gestational age, 28.0 [28.6-29.4] weeks; 174 boys [55.8%]) were randomized to the NCPAP group (153 infants) or the NIPPV group (159 infants).

NIV failure within 72 hours occurred in 40 infants (26.1%) in the NCPAP group vs 21 infants (13.2%) in the NIPPV group (adjusted risk difference, 12.8%; 95% CI, 4.2%-21.6%; P = .004; O'Brien-Fleming adjusted α = .005), exceeding the noninferiority margin and conclusively demonstrating inferiority of NCPAP.
NIV failure within 7 days was also higher in the NCPAP group (42 infants [27.5%] vs 24 infants [15.1%]; risk difference, 12.4%; 95% CI, 3.4%-21.4%; P = .008).
No significant differences were observed between groups for most complications.
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
articles.practiceImpact: Use this for journal club before practice change; decide whether the measured outcome is important enough for bedside decisions.

The confidence interval suggests how much uncertainty remains around the effect estimate.

I would interpret this cautiously because Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat..

englishNotes.speakingPrompt: In one sentence, explain whether Nasal Continuous Positive Airway Pressure should change ICU practice today.

articles.title: Integrated Telehealth Rehabilitation and Quality of Life in Mechanically Ventilated Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
articles.summary: To evaluate the effect of an integrated multicomponent telehealth-based rehabilitation intervention on health-related quality of life at 90 days after hospital discharge among adults with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure requiring invasive mechanical ventilation. Health-related quality of life at 90 days after hospital discharge, measured using the EuroQol 5-Dimension 3-Level (EQ-5D-3L) utility score (range, -0.17 [worse than death] to 1 [best health state], with 0 representing death). Among 1916 enrolled patients (mean [SD] age, 60.6 [17.3] years; 43.6% female), 1063 were assigned to the intervention and 853 to usual care per local protocols. At 90 days after hospital discharge, mean (SD) EQ-5D-3L utility scores were higher in the intervention group than in the usual care group (0.16 [0.31] vs 0.12 [0.28]; adjusted difference, 0.049; 95% CI, 0.0002 to 0.098; P = .04) but did not differ among survivors (0.60 [0.32] vs 0.59 [0.32]; adjusted difference, -0.045; 95% CI, -0.138 to 0.045; P = .34). Compared with usual care, the intervention resulted in lower 90-day all-cause mortality (71.8% [676 of 941] vs 78.3% [584 of 746]; adjusted difference, -7.6%; 95% CI, -14.7% to -0.6%; P = .03) and shorter mean (SD) mechanical ventilation duration (9.9 [10.3] vs 15.5 [15.9] days; adjusted difference, -6.2 days; 95% CI, -8.5 to -3.9; P < .001).
articles.analysis: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

pico.outcomes: Health-related quality of life at 90 days after hospital discharge, measured using the EuroQol 5-Dimension 3-Level (EQ-5D-3L) utility score (range, -0.17 [worse than death] to 1 [best health state], with 0 representing death).

keyResults: Among 1916 enrolled patients (mean [SD] age, 60.6 [17.3] years; 43.6% female), 1063 were assigned to the intervention and 853 to usual care per local protocols.

At 90 days after hospital discharge, mean (SD) EQ-5D-3L utility scores were higher in the intervention group than in the usual care group (0.16 [0.31] vs 0.12 [0.28]; adjusted difference, 0.049; 95% CI, 0.0002 to 0.098; P = .04) but did not differ among survivors (0.60 [0.32] vs 0.59 [0.32]; adjusted difference, -0.045; 95% CI, -0.138 to 0.045; P = .34).

Compared with usual care, the intervention resulted in lower 90-day all-cause mortality (71.8% [676 of 941] vs 78.3% [584 of 746]; adjusted difference, -7.6%; 95% CI, -14.7% to -0.6%; P = .03) and shorter mean (SD) mechanical ventilation duration (9.9 [10.3] vs 15.5 [15.9] days; adjusted difference, -6.2 days; 95% CI, -8.5 to -3.9; P < .001).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.

articles.title: Nutrition support in the ICU: current evidence and evolving standards.
articles.summary: Increasing protein delivery has not consistently improved survival or functional recovery and may even be harmful, especially in patients with acute kidney injury. Nutritional support is a cornerstone of care in critically ill patients, yet the optimal delivery of macronutrient across phases of critical illness remains controversial. During the acute phase, profound metabolic alterations including anabolic resistance, enhanced proteolysis and endogenous energy production contribute to rapid muscle wasting. Recent large randomized trials have challenged the traditional paradigm of early full nutritional replacement, which may cause harm through overfeeding, refeeding syndrome, impaired autophagy, and metabolic intolerance. Low-calorie and low-protein strategies during the first week of critical illness, particularly in mechanically ventilated patients requiring organ support, may improve recovery and reduce complications.
articles.analysis: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

pico.population: critically ill patients, yet the optimal delivery of macronutrient across phases of critical illness remains controversial
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Main outcomes are those reported in the abstract; prioritize patient-centered outcomes such as mortality, ventilation duration, ICU stay, and adverse events.

keyResults: Increasing protein delivery has not consistently improved survival or functional recovery and may even be harmful, especially in patients with acute kidney injury.
Nutritional support is a cornerstone of care in critically ill patients, yet the optimal delivery of macronutrient across phases of critical illness remains controversial.
During the acute phase, profound metabolic alterations including anabolic resistance, enhanced proteolysis and endogenous energy production contribute to rapid muscle wasting.

Recent large randomized trials have challenged the traditional paradigm of early full nutritional replacement, which may cause harm through overfeeding, refeeding syndrome, impaired autophagy, and metabolic intolerance.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
